### Cross-method evaluation of Protein 2 models

#### **Methods**

As in the previous protein, a standardized cross-method evaluation protocol was applied to ensure a robust and unbiased comparison among the different predictive algorithms.

#### **Final model selection**

The universal QMEANDisCo scores (AF2: 0.50; ESMFold: 0.48; SWISS-MODEL: 0.47) reflect the expected penalty from the massive intrinsically disordered N-terminal tail and internal loop, rather than algorithmic failures. However, the QMEAN Z-score clearly highlights the thermodynamic superiority of the multimeric state: while isolated monomers exhibited severe stereochemical strain (Z-scores from -3.43 to -7.24), the AlphaFold3 trimeric assembly completely resolved this tension, drastically improving to -1.44.

Pairwise PyMOL alignments, summarized in @tbl-rmsd-comparisons-protein2, further exposed the limitations of homology modeling in the "Twilight Zone". The structural deviation between the SWISS-MODEL and AF3 trimers was massive (RMSD 6.96 Å across 6,967 aligned atoms), indicating that SWISS-MODEL improperly imposed the multimeric twist of its viral template. This bias is corroborated at the monomeric level, where AF3 and ESMFold yielded much closer alignments against the SWISS-MODEL single chain (RMSDs of 4.05 Å and 4.14 Å, respectively).

Finally, comparing the two AI models (ESMFold vs. AF2 full monomers) yielded a high global RMSD of 7.11 Å (across 3,742 atoms). This variance is heavily driven by diverging random coil trajectories in the flexible tail, but it also perfectly illustrates their algorithmic differences: AlphaFold relies on evolutionary co-variation to accurately predict the fine geometric twist of the parallel beta-helix, whereas ESMFold successfully captures the general topology ab initio, but lacks the evolutionary data for fine-grained structural constraints.

| Pairwise alignment | Structural level | RMSD (Å) |
|---|---|---:|
| SWISS-MODEL vs. AlphaFold 3 | Trimer | 6.96 |
| SWISS-MODEL vs. AlphaFold 3 | Monomer | 4.05 |
| SWISS-MODEL vs. ESMFold | Monomer | 4.14 |
| ESMFold vs. AlphaFold 2 | Monomer | 7.11 |

: RMSD values obtained from pairwise PyMOL alignments. {#tbl-rmsd-comparisons-protein2}

The results of the [VoroMQA](https://bioinformatics.lt/wtsam/voromqa) evaluation are summarized in the @tbl-model-comparison-protein2.

| Model | Global score | Interface i_score | i_energy |
|------|-------------|------------------|---------|
| **AlphaFold 3** | 0.524 | 0.579 | -4028.0 |
| **AlphaFold 2** | 0.496 | - | - |
| **ESMFold** | 0.477 | - | - |
| **Swiss-Model** | 0.410 | 0.304 | -1182.8 |

: Comparison of the structural quality and interface scores obtained for the models generated with AlphaFold3, AlphaFold2, ESMFold and Swiss-Model for Protein 2. {#tbl-model-comparison-protein2}

The evaluated models differ in their oligomeric state, (trimer in AF3/Swiss-Model vs. monomer in AF2/ESMFold). However, VoroMQA analysis allows a normalized assessment of atomic packing quality, allowing comparison across models despite their oligomeric state. 
For this reason, the monomeric models do not have interface-related scores (`i_score` and `i_energy`), as they do not have inter-chain interactions.

The AlphaFold 3 model had the highest global score, as is shown in @tbl-model-comparison-protein2. Its score is notably higher than those obtained for the monomeric models, suggesting that the protein has a more favorable atomic packing when it oligomerizes. 
This observation supports the hypothesis that oligomerization is required to achieve the thermodynamically stable native conformation.

In addition, the Local Score profiles (upper panels in @fig-voromqa-panels-protein2) show a highly consistent structural pattern across all models, where the high-confidence (blue) and low-confidence (red) regions occur at the same positions in the first three models, except for Swiss-Model.
Notably, the AlphaFold models exhibit a higher profile, suggesting that oligomerization stabilizes flexible regions. In contrast, the Swiss-Model profile displays increased noise and local disruptions, reflecting the limitations of template-based modeling under low sequence identity conditions.

::: {#fig-voromqa-panels-protein2 layout-ncol=1}

![a) AlphaFold 3 VoroMQA evaluation](pics/Protein2/VoroMQA/Voro_AF3.png){#a}

![b) AlphaFold 2 VoroMQA evaluation](/pics/Protein2/VoroMQA/Voro_AF2.png){#a}

![c) ESMFold VoroMQA evaluation](/pics/Protein2/VoroMQA/Voro_ESM.png){#c}

![d) Swiss-Model VoroMQA evaluation](/pics/Protein2/VoroMQA/Voro_Swiss.png){#b}


**VoroMQA structural quality assessment for the three predicted models**(AlphaFold 3, AlphaFold 2, ESMFold and Swiss-Model). For each figure, the upper tracks show local structural quality and sequence annotations, while the lower plot shows the residue-wise quality score. Higher values (blue) indicate better predicted local quality, whereas lower values (red) indicate less reliable regions.
:::

Focusing on the trimeric models, AlphaFold 3 model has a higher score in both interface-related metrics.
The interface score of the AlphaFold 3 model is notably higher than the Swiss-Model one, supporting a more realistic and a better interaction surface between subunits.

In addition, the interface energy is significantly more favorable in the AlphaFold 3 model compared to Swiss-Model. The large difference indicates that the interactions predicted by the first server are not only higher but also energetically more stable.

The poorest performance of Swiss-Model compared with the other models can be explained to the limitations of the template-based modeling, which is notable under low sequence identity conditions, as in this particular case. The lack of strong templates in disordered or flexible regions, forces Swiss-Model to eliminate these regions. As a result of the structural restrictions from the template, the interface geometry and the interaction stability are worse.

For all these reasons, the final model selected was the one generated by AlphaFold 3, shown in @fig-protein2-final. It is the most reliable, both globally and at interface level, giving evidence that the trimeric assembly is the more stable state for Protein 2.

::: {#fig-protein2-final layout="[[1,1], [1,1]]"}

![**AlphaFold3, Side View.** Colored by chains](/pics/Protein2/final/chain_side_view.png){#fig-a}

![**AlphaFold3, Top View.** Colored by chains](/pics/Protein2/final/chain_top_view.png){#fig-b}

![**AlphaFold3, Side View.** Colored by secondary structure](/pics/Protein2/final/ss_side_view.png){#fig-c}

![**AlphaFold3, Top View.** Colored by secondary structure](/pics/Protein2/final/ss_top_view.png){#fig-d}

**Final AlphaFold3 model of the Protein 2.** (a) Side view of the structure colored by chains, showing the three subunits that form the channel. (b) Top view colored by chains, highlighting the symmetric arrangement of the trimer. (c) Side view colored by secondary structure, where α-helices are shown in navy blue, β-sheets in green, and loops or disordered regions in red. (d) Top view colored by secondary structure. 
:::
